FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline 9-10 year olds (2016-2018), N=8292 in analysis

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: generalized structural equation modeling (GSEM) with mediation testing

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article, American Journal on Addictions, 2026

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: 'social media time' (named SM variable, IV at Year 2) paired with 'nicotine experimentation' (individual-trait behavioral outcome at Year 4); direct effect β=0.138, 95% CI 0.081-0.196, p<.001 (Table 2)

FT_IC3.evidence: Direct effect coefficient β=0.138 (p<.001) and indirect effect through expectancies β=0.009 (p=.002) reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results: direct β=0.138, indirect β=0.009, proportion mediated 5.97% (95% CI 1.27%-10.67%, p=.013)

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design (Years 2-4) with SM as IV (not DV-only); SM-as-IV analysis present in primary model, so EC7 does not apply
notes: Mediation analysis identifies positive smoking expectancies as pathway; smoking expectancy measured at Year 3 as mediator

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 5 (2021-2023), N=11,530 after multiple imputation

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multivariable linear regression on cross-sectional data

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published research note, BMC Research Notes, 2026

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: 'dating app use' (named social platform variable, IV) paired with 'depression symptoms' (individual-trait mental health outcome, CBCL DSM-5 Affective Problems Scale); adjusted β=0.42, 95% CI 0.08-0.76, p=0.017 (Table 2)

FT_IC3.evidence: Standardized beta coefficient β=0.42 reported with 95% CI and p-value

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative result: adjusted β=0.42 (95% CI 0.08-0.76, p=0.017); sensitivity analyses also yielded β=0.44 (p=0.035) with additional covariates

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (Year 5 only); cross-sectional carve-out applies—EC7 does not apply regardless of any other characteristics
notes: Prevalence of dating app use was 0.9% in sample; sensitivity analyses included cyberbullying, sleep, pubertal status, screen time, parental monitoring, phone ownership age, relationship status

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline (2016-2018), N=3847 after quality control (from 4524 enrolled)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multiple machine learning regression approaches (ridge, LASSO, elastic net, linear regression)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found (no estimates for time on phone, SM platform, texting, app use, etc.). Step 2: Composite 'screen time' defined in methods as 'average minutes per week a child typically spends on a computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device'—this is multi-device aggregated time without per-device breakout. Per binding rule §2.4 clarification 5, multi-device aggregated time fails IC2 unless cellphone-only breakout exists elsewhere. No separate cellphone or SM time estimate reported.

notes: Screen time coefficient was 1.17 (LASSO) and second-most significant in full sample linear regression (p=7.5×10-8), but the composite includes TV, console/PC gaming, computer, tablet, and cellphone—no isolation of phone/SM component
